papers.title: DiffMethylTools: a toolbox for the detection, annotation, and visualization of differential methylation.
papers.abstract: DNA methylation is a fundamental epigenetic mechanism, and its significant changes (i.e., differential methylation) regulate gene expression, cell-type specification, and disease progression without altering the underlying DNA sequence. Differential methylation has traditionally been detected by statistical comparison of two groups using existing tools and has supported a wide range of downstream analyses in human disease studies. However, few toolboxes efficiently integrate robust detection, annotation, and visualization of differential methylation, and the reproducibility of detected signals
papers.summary: DiffMethylTools is a new software toolbox that integrates robust detection, annotation, and visualization of differential DNA methylation from group comparisons. It addresses the gap in existing tools by combining these three key steps into a single, efficient workflow, aiming to improve reproducibility in epigenetic studies.

papers.title: Evidence for cryptic sex in Escovopsis, a mycoparasite in the fungus-growing ant symbiosis.
papers.abstract: The Red Queen hypothesis for the maintenance of sexual reproduction proposes that sex should be favored during parasite-host interactions. The presence of sexual reproduction in Escovopsis sensu lato (s.l.) (Hypocreaceae, Ascomycota), obligate specialized parasites on the fungal gardens of fungus-growing ants, has been debated; previous analyses have concluded that sex is likely absent based on Escovopsis s.l. appearing to lack a complete mating-type (MAT) locus, which controls sexual compatibility in fungi. Using 39 previously sequenced genomes, we found that computational annotation of these
papers.summary: This study provides evidence for cryptic sexual reproduction in Escovopsis, a fungal parasite of fungus-growing ant gardens, challenging previous assumptions that it was asexual. Using genomic analysis of 39 strains, the authors found that Escovopsis likely retains the genetic machinery for sex, supporting the Red Queen hypothesis that host-parasite interactions favor sexual reproduction.

papers.title: Ensaya: An ensemble age model for prediction of chronological age in adolescents and young adults.
papers.abstract: DNA methylation-based models for chronological age estimation have, for over a decade, been among the most important methods for age prediction in humans. While large-scale data have enabled the development of numerous models, their accuracy varies substantially depending on choice of machine learning algorithm, pre-processing pipeline, age range, tissue type, and population characteristics. Accurate age estimation in adolescents and young adults is particularly relevant in legal, investigational, and biomedical contexts. Here, we systematically evaluated blood-based DNA methylation age predic
papers.summary: This study presents Ensaya, an ensemble model that combines multiple machine learning algorithms to improve the accuracy of chronological age prediction from DNA methylation data in adolescents and young adults. The model addresses the challenge of variable accuracy across different methods, offering a more reliable tool for legal, investigational, and biomedical applications in this specific age group.

papers.title: Haplotype-aware long-read error correction.
papers.abstract: Error correction of long reads is an important initial step in genome assembly workflows. For organisms with ploidy greater than one, it is important to preserve haplotype-specific variation during read correction. This challenge has driven the development of several haplotype-aware correction methods. However, existing methods are based on either ad-hoc heuristics or deep learning approaches. In this paper, we introduce a rigorous formulation for this problem. Our approach builds on the minimum error correction framework used in reference-based haplotype phasing. We prove that the proposed fo
papers.summary: This paper presents a new rigorous mathematical formulation for error-correcting long sequencing reads while preserving haplotype-specific variation in polyploid genomes, addressing a key challenge in genome assembly. The method builds on the minimum error correction framework from haplotype phasing, offering a principled alternative to existing ad-hoc heuristics or deep learning approaches.

papers.title: Programmable aging function-related mitochondrial DNA 5-methylcytosine (m5C) modification with a TALE-directed methyltransferase.
papers.abstract: Mitochondrial epigenetic editing offers a potential strategy for modulating disease-causing mitochondrial genes while leaving the underlying DNA sequence unchanged. In this study, we present MEE, a mitochondrial epigenetic editor consisting of mitochondrion-targeted TALE modules fused to Dnmt3A and Dnmt3L methyltransferases. MEE efficiently directed site-specific 5mC methylation within cellular mitochondrial DNA with low detectable off-target activity under the tested conditions. MEE-mediated methylation at the C12191 (H) site exceeded 53% and was associated with an approximately 95% reduction
papers.summary: Researchers developed a programmable tool called MEE that can add specific chemical marks (5-methylcytosine) to mitochondrial DNA without changing the underlying genetic sequence. This tool achieved over 53% methylation at a target site in mitochondrial DNA, leading to a 95% reduction in gene expression, offering a potential new approach for modulating mitochondrial genes in aging and disease.

papers.title: Epigenetic dysregulation in osteonecrosis of the femoral head: a critical review of DNA methylation, histone modifications, and clinical translation.
papers.abstract: Osteonecrosis of the Femoral Head (ONFH) is a progressive osteoarticular disease caused by local ischemia, ultimately leading to femoral head collapse and loss of joint function. Epigenetics has emerged as a pivotal tool for unraveling the complex pathogenesis of ONFH, with DNA methylation and histone modifications playing central roles. These key epigenetic mechanisms critically regulate osteocyte function, bone remodeling, and angiogenesis. Aberrant DNA methylation silences osteogenic genes, e.g., runt-related transcription factor 2(RUNX2), bone morphogenetic protein 2(BMP2) and angiogenic f
papers.journal: Journal of orthopaedic surgery and research
papers.summary: This review examines how DNA methylation and histone modifications contribute to osteonecrosis of the femoral head (ONFH) by silencing key osteogenic and angiogenic genes. The authors highlight the potential for epigenetic therapies to improve diagnosis and treatment of this progressive joint disease.

papers.title: Fine mapping of PTSD GWAS reveals a role for amygdala Foxp2 in regulation of fear and threat responses.
papers.abstract: Post-traumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD) is a debilitating psychiatric condition caused by severe trauma exposure and characterized by ongoing dysregulation of fear processing, hyperarousal, and amygdala activation, but with limited effective treatments. Recent large-scale genome-wide association studies (GWAS) of PTSD have identified the transcription factor FOXP2 as a highly-significant, top putative risk gene. Both fine-mapping of the PTSD GWAS with amygdala-specific expression quantitative trait loci (eQTL) data, and transcriptome-wide association analyses, show that altered expression of FOX
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: This study identifies the transcription factor FOXP2 as a key risk gene for PTSD by integrating GWAS data with amygdala-specific gene expression analyses, and demonstrates that altered Foxp2 expression in the amygdala regulates fear and threat responses. The findings provide a mechanistic link between genetic risk and the neural circuitry underlying PTSD symptoms.

papers.title: Novel Drosophila cis -regulatory elements can be uncovered by footprinting transcription factor binding sites in ATAC-seq data.
papers.abstract: Developmental processes depend on carefully coordinated gene expression. Expression is modulated by the binding of transcription factors (TFs) to cis-regulatory elements (CREs), like enhancers and promoters. Many computational and experimental approaches have been developed to find CREs, particularly enhancers, in the genome, each with strengths and caveats. Given the increasing availability of ATAC-seq data and methods to find TF binding therein, we hypothesized that we could use TF footprinting tools to find clusters of TF binding events within accessible chromatin that may act as CREs. Usin
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: This study presents a computational approach to identify cis-regulatory elements (CREs) in Drosophila by analyzing transcription factor binding footprints from ATAC-seq data. The method leverages the increasing availability of ATAC-seq datasets to detect clusters of TF binding events within accessible chromatin, potentially uncovering novel enhancers and promoters. This offers a cost-effective alternative to traditional experimental methods for mapping regulatory elements.

papers.title: kmerRRR: A k-mer based tool for functional genomics in Repeat Rich Regions.
papers.abstract: Highly repetitive sequences pose problems for genome assembly and analysis. While advances in long-read sequencing technologies have helped reveal the organization of repetitive genomic sequences at unprecedented resolution, their functional characterization remains difficult because molecular assays that probe protein-DNA interactions and characterize expression often rely on short read sequencing. The repetitive nature of these regions poses major challenges for methods relying on sequence mapping, which is exacerbated for short reads. Repetitive genome regions often have low mappability, le
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: This paper introduces kmerRRR, a k-mer based tool designed to analyze functional genomics data in repetitive genomic regions, which are typically difficult to study with short-read sequencing due to low mappability. By using k-mers instead of traditional read mapping, the tool enables characterization of protein-DNA interactions and expression in repeat-rich regions. This addresses a key gap in functional genomics, as repetitive sequences are often excluded from standard analyses despite their biological importance.

papers.title: Enhanced plant bottom-up histone proteomics.
papers.abstract: The correct tools for characterization of histone proteoforms are essential for deciphering plant epigenetic mechanisms and their subsequent application in biotechnology. Insights into the epigenetic landscape of plant chromatin can be advanced using bottom-up proteomics. MS analysis of histone peptides relies on careful sample preparation, including chemical derivatization of amine groups prior to MS to improve their chromatographic behaviour during nanoHPLC separation. Characterizing histones in plant tissues remains especially challenging due to the presence of diverse, species-specific com
papers.summary: This paper discusses the challenges and solutions for characterizing histone proteoforms in plants using bottom-up proteomics, emphasizing the importance of proper sample preparation and chemical derivatization for mass spectrometry analysis. It provides methodological guidance to advance understanding of plant epigenetic mechanisms, which is crucial for biotechnological applications.

papers.title: Catalytic footprints of DNMT1 dynamics during mammalian cell state transitions.
papers.abstract: 5-methylcytosine is a reversible regulatory mark in mammalian genomes whose distribution varies across genetic loci, cell types, and disease conditions. Cytosine-5 methylation is brought about by three AdoMet-dependent DNA methyltransferases (DNMTs) with primary tasks in de novo (DNMT3A and DNMT3B) or maintenance (DNMT1) methylation. However, the precise roles and interplay between the three methylation "writers" remain obscure. To selectively track an individual DNMT, we derived a heterozygous mESC line with chromosomal alleles expressing the wild-type and a sterically engineered variant of D
papers.summary: This study investigates the specific roles of DNA methyltransferase 1 (DNMT1) in mammalian cell state transitions by engineering a mouse embryonic stem cell line that allows selective tracking of DNMT1 activity. The findings reveal distinct catalytic footprints of DNMT1 during cell state changes, shedding light on its interplay with other DNMTs in maintaining and modifying DNA methylation patterns.

papers.title: Pharmacogenomics and Epigenetic Regulation Transforming Pediatric Precision Therapeutics.
papers.abstract: Pediatric drug therapy remains fundamentally challenged by profound interindividual variability driven by dynamic development, genetic, and environmental factors. Although dosing strategies based on age, body weight, or body surface area remain important starting points in pediatric pharmacotherapy, they may not fully capture ontogeny-dependent variability in drug disposition and response. Consequently, clinically relevant differences in efficacy and toxicity may still occur among children receiving similar weight-adjusted doses. Pharmacogenomics offers a promising framework for individualized
papers.summary: This review discusses how pharmacogenomics and epigenetic regulation can improve pediatric precision medicine by accounting for developmental, genetic, and environmental factors that cause variability in drug response among children. It highlights the limitations of current weight-based dosing strategies and proposes that integrating genomic and epigenetic data could lead to more effective and safer treatments.

papers.title: Identification of compounds that repress DUX4 expression in facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy.
papers.abstract: Facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy (FSHD) is caused by epigenetic dysregulation of the disease locus, leading to pathogenic misexpression of DUX4 in skeletal muscle. Thus, most FSHD therapeutic approaches target DUX4. Our previous study identified the chromatin remodeling factor BAZ1A (bromodomain adjacent to zinc finger domain protein 1A) as a promising target for therapeutic development. Here we used an artificial intelligence-based screening pipeline to identify molecules predicted to bind the BAZ1A bromodomain, and validated hit compounds using FSHD-specific assays in FSHD myocytes. On
papers.summary: This study used an AI-based screening pipeline to identify compounds predicted to bind the BAZ1A bromodomain, a chromatin remodeling factor implicated in DUX4 misexpression in facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy (FSHD). The hit compounds were validated in FSHD myocytes, showing potential for repressing DUX4 expression and offering a new therapeutic avenue for FSHD.

papers.title: Epigenetic compartmentalization of mitotic chromosomes by phase-separation-driven repulsion between WDR5 and the chromosomal passenger complex.
papers.abstract: Histone modifications recruit chromatin regulators, but how their spatial patterns are established on mitotic chromosomes remains unclear. Spatial separation between active chromatin and centromeric signaling is essential for accurate chromosome segregation, yet the physical principles linking epigenetic marks to mitotic protein organization are poorly defined. Here, we show that liquid-liquid phase separation (LLPS) of WDR5, a core component of H3K4 methyltransferase complexes, acts as a spatial organizer of mitotic chromosomes. WDR5 LLPS is driven by multivalent charged and hydrophobic inter
papers.summary: This study reveals that the protein WDR5 forms liquid-like droplets on mitotic chromosomes through phase separation, and these droplets physically repel the chromosomal passenger complex (CPC) to create distinct active and centromeric chromatin zones. This phase-separation-driven repulsion mechanism explains how epigenetic marks spatially organize proteins during cell division to ensure accurate chromosome segregation.

papers.title: Epigenetic lockdown of type I interferon sensing and signalling in human pluripotent cells.
papers.abstract: The Human Silencing Hub (HUSH) complex safeguards genome integrity in human somatic cells, repressing transposable elements and regulating type I interferon (IFN-I) induction. Here, we use depletion of MPP8 in human induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs) as a tool to investigate epigenetic control of the IFN-I system in early development. We confirmed that human iPSCs display an attenuated IFN-I pathway, whereas iPSC-derived neural progenitor cells (NPCs) respond robustly to IFN-I pathway agonists. We found that, in iPSCs, depletion of MPP8 was sufficient to induce expression of young LINE-1 e
papers.summary: This study reveals that the HUSH complex epigenetically silences type I interferon sensing and signaling in human pluripotent stem cells, explaining their attenuated immune response. Depletion of MPP8 in iPSCs reactivates young LINE-1 elements and interferon pathway genes, while differentiated neural progenitor cells regain robust interferon responsiveness. These findings provide insight into how early developmental cells avoid inflammatory responses to transposable elements.

papers.title: Population-scale detection of methylation outliers from long-read genome sequencing.
papers.abstract: Aberrant DNA methylation can mediate the functional effects of rare genetic variation and contribute to imprinting disorders, repeat expansion diseases, and other pathogenic regulatory mechanisms. Long-read sequencing technologies now enable genome-wide detection of CpG methylation alongside genetic variation from a single assay. However, methods for systematic identification and interpretation of methylation outliers from long-read sequencing data remain limited.
papers.journal: medRxiv : the preprint server for health sciences
papers.summary: This paper presents a new method for detecting DNA methylation outliers from long-read sequencing data at a population scale, enabling the identification of aberrant methylation patterns that may underlie rare genetic disorders. The approach leverages the ability of long-read sequencing to simultaneously detect genetic variation and CpG methylation, filling a gap in tools for systematic analysis of methylation outliers. This work has potential applications in diagnosing imprinting disorders, repeat expansion diseases, and other conditions with epigenetic contributions.

papers.title: Application of plant viruses in biotechnology with relation to artificial intelligence, machine learning, and nanotechnology.
papers.abstract: Plant viruses, once viewed as harmful agricultural pathogens, are now powerful tools in biotechnology. Their nanoscale structure, self-assembly, and biocompatibility enable applications in agriculture, medicine, and environmental sustainability. They serve in gene delivery, genome editing, diagnostics, and nanomaterials for vaccines and drug delivery. Integration with AI, ML, and bioinformatics enhances virus discovery and prediction. Despite challenges, plant viruses are emerging as versatile, sustainable resources for global biotechnological innovations.
papers.summary: This review highlights how plant viruses are being repurposed from agricultural threats into versatile biotechnological tools, leveraging their nanoscale structure for applications in gene delivery, diagnostics, and nanomaterials. The integration of artificial intelligence and machine learning is accelerating virus discovery and predictive modeling, positioning plant viruses as sustainable resources for innovation in medicine and agriculture.

papers.title: GenART: Reading the genome's language with adaptive "words".
papers.abstract: Tokenization is both a prerequisite and a central challenge for genomic language models, due to the inherent difficulty of delineating meaningful segments within continuous DNA sequences. We present GenART, a genomic language model framework that dynamically segments DNA into variable-length words directly from raw sequences without manual annotation. Its key adaptive tokenization module infers biologically meaningful boundaries from multi-scale contextual signals during pretraining. GenART demonstrates competitive performance across diverse tasks, outperforming leading approaches. Word bounda
papers.summary: GenART is a new genomic language model that learns to automatically segment DNA sequences into variable-length 'words' without needing manual annotation, addressing a key challenge in genomic tokenization. By dynamically inferring biologically meaningful boundaries from context during training, it outperforms existing methods across multiple tasks. This adaptive approach could improve how AI models understand and analyze genomic data.

papers.title: Context-aware simulation enables systematic optimization of long-read mapping parameters.
papers.abstract: Long-read mapping performance is critical for downstream genomic analyses but remains sensitive to parameter selection. We present CycSim, a context-aware long-read simulator that learns sequence-context-dependent error profiles from empirical data and generates realistic simulated reads. CycSim more faithfully recapitulated real long-read characteristics than existing simulators, providing a high-fidelity simulation framework with known ground truth. Using this framework, we identified a Cyclone-specific parameter set that achieved 2.78-fold faster mapping than an ONT-oriented baseline while
papers.summary: CycSim is a new long-read simulator that learns realistic error patterns from real data, enabling more accurate benchmarking of mapping tools. Using CycSim, researchers identified optimized parameters that made long-read mapping nearly three times faster without sacrificing accuracy.

papers.title: TNEAtlas: A Pan-cancer Database to Identify and Characterize Transcribed Non-coding Elements.
papers.abstract: Advances in precision oncology have underscored the need to move beyond regulatory frameworks centered on protein-coding regions and better understand regulatory mechanisms within the non-coding genome. To systematically characterize transcribed non-coding elements (TNEs) in cancer, we developed an automated computational framework that integrates 130 RNA-seq datasets from 26 cancer types and 16 human tissues and identifies more than 2 million intergenic TNEs. In parallel, we established the first pan-cancer TNE database, TNEAtlas, which annotates TNEs with epigenetic signatures and functional
papers.summary: Researchers created TNEAtlas, a comprehensive database cataloging over 2 million transcribed non-coding elements (TNEs) across 26 cancer types by analyzing 130 RNA-seq datasets. This resource systematically characterizes these previously understudied genomic regions and annotates them with epigenetic and functional information, providing a valuable tool for understanding non-coding regulatory mechanisms in cancer.

papers.title: AI-driven neoantigen identification: a comprehensive review from somatic variant calling to T cell recognition.
papers.abstract: Neoantigens-tumor-specific peptides generated by somatic mutations-are central targets of effective anticancer T cell immunity and underpin the clinical success of immune checkpoint blockade and personalized cancer vaccines. Advances in high-throughput sequencing, immunopeptidomics, and artificial intelligence (AI) have transformed neoantigen discovery from tailored experimental workflows into scalable, computational pipelines. However, accurately identifying the small subset of tumor mutations that yield processed, presented, and immunogenic epitopes remains a major bottleneck.
papers.summary: This review examines how artificial intelligence is transforming neoantigen discovery by integrating high-throughput sequencing, immunopeptidomics, and computational pipelines to identify tumor-specific peptides that trigger T cell immunity. Despite these advances, accurately pinpointing which mutations produce immunogenic epitopes remains a key challenge for personalized cancer vaccines and immune checkpoint therapies.

papers.title: High-accuracy SNV calling for bacterial isolates using deep learning with AccuSNV.
papers.abstract: Accurate detection of mutations within bacterial species is critical for fundamental studies of microbial evolution, reconstructing transmission events, and identifying antimicrobial resistance mutations. While many tools have been developed to identify single nucleotide variants (SNVs) from whole-genome sequencing, they often suffer from high false positive rates due to the complexity of bacterial genomes and the need for different filtering cutoffs across sample types and sequencing depths. As datasets increase in size, the manual filtering required for high accuracy presents a significant o
papers.summary: AccuSNV is a deep learning tool that improves the accuracy of detecting single nucleotide variants in bacterial genomes, reducing false positives that plague existing methods. It eliminates the need for manual filtering by learning to distinguish true mutations from sequencing errors across diverse sample types and depths.

papers.title: OMIDIENT: Multiomics Integration for Cancer by Dirichlet Auto-Encoder Networks.
papers.abstract: To achieve a more comprehensive understanding of cancer, novel computational methods are required for the integrative analysis of data from different molecular layers, such as genomics, transcriptomics, and epigenomics. Here, we present an innovative multi-omics integrative method that performs unsupervised representation learning, referred to as OMIDIENT: multiOMics Integration for cancer by DIrichlet auto-ENcoder neTworks. OMIDIENT provides a natural framework for modeling sparse and compositional latent representations by employing a deep generative model, where the latent space is distribu
papers.summary: OMIDIENT is a new deep learning method that integrates different types of molecular data (genomics, transcriptomics, epigenomics) to better understand cancer. It uses a Dirichlet auto-encoder to learn sparse and interpretable representations of the data without needing labels, which could improve cancer subtyping and biomarker discovery.

papers.title: RankVar: machine learning-based variant ranking and reinterpretation for rare genetic diseases.
papers.abstract: Prior biological knowledge and phenotype information can help identify disease genes from whole genome/exome sequencing studies, but how best to incorporate external knowledge with variant data remains challenging. We developed a machine learning algorithm called RankVar to prioritize causative variants for rare diseases, based on clinical notes and genome/exome sequencing profiles.
papers.summary: RankVar is a new machine learning tool that combines clinical notes with genome/exome sequencing data to better prioritize disease-causing variants for rare genetic diseases. It addresses the challenge of integrating prior biological knowledge and phenotype information to improve variant interpretation.

papers.title: Somatic mobility of transposons is explosive and shaped by distinct integration biases in Arabidopsis thaliana.
papers.abstract: The movement of transposable elements (TEs) in somatic cells generates genetic and phenotypic diversity across the soma of individual organisms. This process is especially important in plants that display a sessile lifestyle, can regenerate from somatic cells, and continuously establish their germline from somatic stem cells. Yet, the scale and integration biases by which TEs may shape the plant soma remain unknown.
papers.summary: This study reveals that transposable elements (TEs) move extensively in somatic cells of Arabidopsis thaliana, generating substantial genetic diversity within individual plants. The integration of TEs is non-random, with distinct biases that shape the plant's somatic genome in ways that may have evolutionary and developmental consequences.

papers.title: METEOR: a data-adaptive Mendelian randomization method for powerful detection of shared and specific exposures underlying multiple outcomes.
papers.abstract: Accurate identification of causal exposures for multimorbidity can benefit the co-prevention and co-management of multiple-related outcomes. This goal can be conceptually addressed within a multi-outcome Mendelian randomization (MR) framework. However, existing multi-outcome MR methods suffer from restrictions on format and availability of data inputs, fail to account for the potential sample overlap, rely on pre-selected independent instrumental variables (IVs), and are unable to account for horizontal pleiotropy. Here, we propose METEOR, a novel MR method that jointly models one exposure and
papers.summary: METEOR is a new Mendelian randomization method that can simultaneously analyze multiple health outcomes to identify which exposures are shared or specific to each outcome, even when study samples overlap. It overcomes key limitations of existing methods by being more flexible with data inputs and accounting for horizontal pleiotropy, making it a practical tool for studying the causes of multimorbidity.

papers.title: Protocol for constructing multi-ancestry polygenic models using S4-Multi.
papers.abstract: Here, we present a protocol to construct multi-ancestry polygenic models using S4-Multi and generate polygenic risk scores. We describe steps for preparing reference genotype files, formatting genome-wide association stud (GWAS) summary statistics, and selecting SNPs using S4_select. We then perform Bayesian shrinkage with S4_shrink and describe procedures for polygenic score generation and performance evaluation. This workflow supports single- and multi-ancestry analyses and uses linkage disequilibrium information to improve prediction accuracy. For complete details on the use and execution o
papers.summary: This protocol provides a step-by-step guide for building multi-ancestry polygenic risk score models using the S4-Multi tool, which leverages linkage disequilibrium information to improve prediction accuracy across diverse populations. The workflow covers data preparation, SNP selection, Bayesian shrinkage, and performance evaluation for both single- and multi-ancestry analyses.

papers.title: Recent advances in the detection and functional analysis of circRNAs with short-read RNA sequencing-based methods.
papers.abstract: Circular RNAs (circRNAs) were first identified approximately 50 years ago in pathogenic viroids as single-stranded, covalently closed RNA molecules. Initially considered by-products of splicing, circRNAs are now recognised as an important class of regulatory RNAs involved in microRNA sponging, RNA-protein interactions, and cellular pathways. Their closed-loop structure, generated through backsplicing, confers resistance to exonucleolytic degradation and contributes to their stability. Owing to their tissue- and disease-specific expression, circRNAs have emerged as promising biomarkers for canc
papers.summary: This review summarizes recent advances in detecting and functionally analyzing circular RNAs (circRNAs) using short-read RNA sequencing methods. It highlights how circRNAs, once considered splicing by-products, are now recognized as stable regulatory molecules with roles in microRNA sponging and protein interactions, and as promising cancer biomarkers.

papers.title: MPOInt: An Epigenetics-Driven Workflow for Integrative Multi-Level Proteomics.
papers.abstract: Proteomics provides key insights into systems regulating cell behavior and can elucidate key epigenetic states influencing biological functions in health and disease. Integrating different modalities of proteomics (total, phospho, epi) allows for a comprehensive understanding of how systems change in response to a stimulus. A current limitation is that these data are often captured individually with a limited scope of how systems regulating biological processes interact. Integrative analyses are essential to accurately depict biological states but often require custom, time-consuming bioinform
papers.summary: This paper presents MPOInt, a new computational workflow that integrates multiple types of proteomics data (total, phospho, and epigenetic) to provide a more complete picture of how cellular systems respond to stimuli. The tool addresses the common problem of analyzing these data types separately, which limits understanding of how biological systems interact. MPOInt offers a streamlined, automated solution for integrative multi-level proteomics analysis.

papers.title: Identification and functional characterization of regulatory variants in DPP9 associated with COVID-19 severity.
papers.abstract: Severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) infection leads to a wide-range of clinical outcomes, which have been extensively studied through genome-wide association studies (GWAS).
papers.summary: This study identifies specific genetic variants in the DPP9 gene that are associated with severe COVID-19 outcomes, and functionally characterizes these variants to show they affect gene regulation. The findings provide mechanistic insight into how genetic differences can influence disease severity.

papers.title: An interpretable deep learning framework uncovers features governing CRISPR-Cas9 genome-editing efficiency.
papers.abstract: CRISPR-Cas9 genome-editing efficiency is strongly influenced by the sequence composition and positional context of single-guide RNAs (sgRNAs). Although numerous deep learning-based models have been developed to predict Cas9 efficiency from sgRNA sequences, most operate as black boxes, offering limited insight into the sequence determinants underlying Cas9 activity. In addition, previous studies often overlook how the positional context of sequence motifs within sgRNAs influences their effects on Cas9 binding or cleavage.
papers.summary: This paper presents an interpretable deep learning model that not only predicts CRISPR-Cas9 editing efficiency from sgRNA sequences but also reveals which sequence features and their positions within the sgRNA are most important for activity. Unlike previous black-box models, this approach provides clear insights into the sequence determinants of Cas9 function, potentially improving guide RNA design.

papers.title: KCFtools: rapid alignment-free method for introgression screening and GWAS using k-mer profiles.
papers.abstract: In the era of multiple genome references, researchers often align sequencing reads against distinct assemblies or even multiple references simultaneously. This enables applications such as the detection of introgressed segments or highly variable genomic regions, which are especially prevalent in large-genome crop species such as lettuce or wheat. However, these applications come at the cost of increased computational burden, inconsistencies in mapping methods, and reduced reproducibility across studies. To address these limitations, we developed KCFtools, a Java-based toolkit that identifies
papers.summary: KCFtools is a new Java-based toolkit that uses k-mer profiles for rapid alignment-free screening of introgressed genomic segments and genome-wide association studies (GWAS). It reduces computational burden and improves reproducibility compared to traditional alignment-based methods, particularly for large-genome crops like lettuce and wheat.

papers.title: DNA methylation clock in bull sperm cells reveals the epigenetic aging characteristics and impact on fertility.
papers.abstract: Aging is a crucial factor influencing semen quality and fertility in bulls, with potential implications for reproductive management and genetic improvement programs. Understanding the molecular mechanisms underlying sperm cell aging is essential for assessing reproductive potential and improving livestock productivity.
papers.journal: Journal of animal science and biotechnology
papers.summary: This study develops a DNA methylation clock specifically for bull sperm cells, revealing how epigenetic aging affects sperm quality and fertility. The findings provide a molecular tool to assess reproductive potential in bulls, which could improve livestock breeding programs.

papers.title: Decoding Viral Dark Matter: Metagenomic Prokaryotic Virus Characterization With Pharokka, Phold, and Phynteny.
papers.abstract: Viral metagenomics is an increasingly powerful tool for understanding the function and structure of viruses across the diverse environments of our planet. However, decoding the functional potential of prokaryotic viral metagenomes is extremely challenging. Pharokka, Phold, and Phynteny are complementary open-source prokaryotic viral genome annotation tools that utilize a variety of bioinformatics approaches to maximally annotate viral metagenomes. This article describes a protocol for installing and running these tools on a viral metagenomic dataset, followed by visualization of annotations us
papers.summary: This protocol paper describes how to use three complementary open-source tools—Pharokka, Phold, and Phynteny—to annotate prokaryotic viral genomes from metagenomic data, addressing the challenge of decoding 'viral dark matter.' It provides step-by-step instructions for installation, running the tools, and visualizing annotations, making viral metagenomics more accessible to researchers.

papers.title: Multi-omics triangulation identifies complement factor H as a genetically supported protective factor in IgA nephropathy.
papers.abstract: IgA nephropathy is the most prevalent primary glomerulonephritis globally. Dysregulation of the complement system, specifically the alternative pathway, is a critical driver of its pathogenesis. While genome-wide association studies consistently map a primary susceptibility locus to the complement factor H gene cluster, extensive linkage disequilibrium and complex structural variations have historically obscured precise causal targets.
papers.summary: This study uses a multi-omics approach to identify complement factor H as a protective factor in IgA nephropathy, clarifying the genetic basis of the disease. By triangulating data from genomics, transcriptomics, and proteomics, the researchers pinpointed complement factor H as a key player in the alternative complement pathway, which is crucial for disease progression. This finding helps resolve previous ambiguities caused by complex genetic variations in the complement factor H gene cluster.

papers.title: Novel DMD Frameshift Variant (p.Leu2017Profs*5) in Spectrin-Like Repeat 16 Expands the Mutational Spectrum of DMD.
papers.abstract: Duchenne muscular dystrophy (DMD) is an X-linked neuromuscular disorder caused by pathogenic variants in the DMD gene, which encodes dystrophin, a cytoskeletal protein linking intracellular actin to the extracellular matrix via the dystrophin-associated protein complex and maintaining muscle fiber integrity. With the emergence of disease-modifying therapies, early and accurate molecular diagnosis is increasingly important. Whole-exome sequencing (WES) is widely used to evaluate unexplained hyperCKemia and distinguish DMD from other inherited neuromuscular disorders.
papers.summary: This paper reports a novel frameshift mutation in the DMD gene (p.Leu2017Profs*5) found in a patient with Duchenne muscular dystrophy, expanding the known mutational spectrum of the disease. The discovery highlights the importance of whole-exome sequencing for accurate molecular diagnosis, especially as new therapies become available.

papers.title: Global distribution of isoprenoid quinones across Bacteria.
papers.abstract: Isoprenoid quinones are redox-active lipids essential for numerous cellular processes, including ATP synthesis via electron transport chains. They are found across all domains of life. Their diversity among microbial taxa has established them as chemotaxonomic markers, while recent studies have linked quinone repertoire variations to metabolic adaptations, and have identified novel quinone types. Despite this renewed interest, a comprehensive overview of quinone distribution across Bacteria is missing. Here, we systematically annotated quinone biosynthetic pathways for major quinones (ubiquino
papers.summary: This study provides the first comprehensive overview of isoprenoid quinone distribution across bacterial taxa by systematically annotating their biosynthetic pathways. The findings reveal that quinone repertoires vary widely across lineages, with implications for understanding metabolic adaptations and microbial ecology.

papers.title: DeepMethylation: A deep learning framework for tissue-specific DNA methylation prediction and functional variant annotation.
papers.abstract: DNA methylation is a key epigenetic modification that regulates gene expression and plays a vital role in cell differentiation, development, and tumorigenesis. However, large-scale experimental profiling of genome-wide DNA methylation remains time-consuming and limited in coverage. We present DeepMethylation, a deep learning framework that integrates DNA sequence and tissue-specific epigenomic features to predict CpG methylation status across the genome. DeepMethylation achieves state-of-the-art performance (average AUROC 0.909) across tissues, accurately imputes methylation beyond array-cover
papers.summary: DeepMethylation is a new deep learning tool that predicts DNA methylation patterns across different tissues by combining DNA sequence information with tissue-specific epigenomic features. It achieves high accuracy (average AUROC 0.909) and can impute methylation levels beyond standard array coverage, offering a cost-effective alternative to large-scale experimental profiling.

papers.title: Deep learning in tumour genomics: from multi-omics integration to precision oncology.
papers.abstract: Cancer remains a leading cause of death globally, with nearly 10 million deaths in 2020. Advances in genomic technologies have revolutionized cancer research, shifting focus towards precision medicine based on comprehensive tumour genomic profiling. Concurrently, deep learning (DL) has emerged as a powerful paradigm for complex biological data. This review critically assesses recent advances in DL applications for tumour genomics, emphasizing four key domains: DNA sequencing analysis for mutation detection, gene expression profiling for cancer subtype classification, methylation function predi
papers.summary: This review critically assesses recent advances in deep learning applications in tumour genomics, covering mutation detection, gene expression profiling, and methylation analysis for precision oncology. It highlights how deep learning integrates multi-omics data to improve cancer diagnosis, classification, and treatment personalization.

papers.title: A survey on forensic age estimation through DNA methylation: recent progress and future directions towards legal age estimation.
papers.abstract: DNA methylation (DNAm) has emerged as one of the most promising molecular biomarkers for age estimation (AE), offering a powerful complement to traditional anthropological and radiological methods. Its potential spans forensic, biomedical, and humanitarian domains, particularly in the identification of deceased individuals and the estimation of legal age in living persons. Despite its rapid development, the forensic implementation of DNAm-based AE models remains challenged by issues of validation, dataset diversity, and tissue-specific variability. This survey provides an up-to-date overview o
papers.summary: This review examines the use of DNA methylation as a biomarker for estimating age in forensic contexts, highlighting its potential to complement traditional methods for identifying deceased individuals and determining legal age in living persons. The authors note that while DNA methylation-based age estimation has advanced rapidly, challenges remain in validation, dataset diversity, and tissue-specific variability that must be addressed for practical forensic implementation.

papers.title: Integrative single-cell and bulk transcriptomics analysis reveals cellular heterogeneity, immune microenvironment dynamics, and prognostic signatures in oral squamous cell carcinoma.
papers.abstract: Oral squamous cell carcinoma (OSCC) represents a significant global health burden with heterogeneous clinical outcomes. Understanding the molecular and cellular complexity of OSCC is crucial for developing precision therapeutic strategies.
papers.summary: This study combines single-cell and bulk transcriptomics to map the cellular heterogeneity and immune microenvironment of oral squamous cell carcinoma (OSCC), identifying distinct cell subtypes and prognostic signatures. The findings provide a detailed molecular landscape that could inform precision therapies for OSCC patients.

papers.title: Study Protocol for a Randomized Controlled Trial of Multisensory Early Oral Administration of Human Milk (M-MILK) for Very Preterm Infants: Enhancing Stress Regulation, Neurodevelopment, and Oral Feeding Skills.
papers.abstract: Background. Early life stress results in disrupted stress regulation, and less optimal neurodevelopment and oral feeding skills in very preterm infants. Despite these associations, there are few evidence-based interventions to help these fragile infants during critical stages of development. Purpose. This article describes an ongoing research protocol of a randomized controlled trial (RCT) to evaluate the multisensory early oral administration of human milk (M-MILK) as an early NICU intervention to improve stress regulation, support optimal neurodevelopment, and promote competent oral feeding
papers.summary: This paper describes the protocol for a randomized controlled trial testing whether early, multisensory oral administration of human milk (M-MILK) can reduce stress, improve neurodevelopment, and enhance oral feeding skills in very preterm infants in the NICU. The study aims to provide an evidence-based intervention for these vulnerable infants during critical developmental stages.

papers.title: Human gut flagellome profiling using FlaPro reveals TLR5-related phenotype-specific alterations in IBD.
papers.abstract: Flagellin, the structural protein of bacterial flagella, activates the innate immune receptor Toll-like receptor 5 (TLR5). However, the ability of different flagellins to bind and stimulate TLR5 varies widely, suggesting that the composition of an individual's flagellin repertoire, defined as flagellome, may influence host-microbiome interactions and inflammation. Here, we developed FlaPro, a computational pipeline for quantification and functional annotation of human gut flagellomes. Functional categories in FlaPro are derived from a machine learning model trained on experimentally characteri
papers.summary: The authors developed FlaPro, a computational pipeline that quantifies and functionally annotates the collection of flagellin proteins (the 'flagellome') in the human gut microbiome. Using this tool, they found that individuals with inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) have distinct flagellome profiles with altered TLR5-stimulating potential, suggesting that the specific flagellins present in a person's gut may influence inflammation and disease phenotype.

papers.title: Nuclear mechanotransduction: tools for mechanical perturbation and chromatin characterization.
papers.abstract: Mechanical cues, ranging from matrix mechanical properties to dynamic mechanical loading, can be transmitted via structural proteins and signaling molecules to the nucleus to reorganize nuclear architecture and modulate chromatin accessibility. This mechanical regulation plays an important role in tissue regeneration and disease development. To gain deeper insights into the mechanical regulation of chromatin organization, it is essential to develop technologies that can apply mechanical inputs and characterize the resulting changes in nuclear structure and chromatin organization. Here, we revi
papers.summary: This review discusses how mechanical cues from the environment are transmitted to the nucleus to reorganize chromatin and influence gene expression, playing key roles in tissue regeneration and disease. The authors highlight the need for advanced technologies to apply mechanical inputs and measure the resulting changes in nuclear structure and chromatin organization.

papers.title: Epigenetic regulation of vertebrate limb development: from field specification to tissue morphogenesis.
papers.abstract: Vertebrate limb development provides an excellent model for understanding how epigenetic mechanisms coordinate the integration of positional information and temporal signals to generate complex three-dimensional structures. An increasing body of evidence shows that chromatin-based mechanisms, including DNA methylation, histone modifications, and higher-order chromatin organization, define the competence of progenitor cells to respond to morphogenetic signals with spatial and temporal precision during limb development. This review explores how epigenetic mechanisms orchestrate and regulate the
papers.summary: This review discusses how epigenetic mechanisms such as DNA methylation, histone modifications, and chromatin organization coordinate positional and temporal signals during vertebrate limb development, from early field specification to final tissue morphogenesis. It highlights how these chromatin-based processes define progenitor cell competence to respond to morphogenetic signals with spatial and temporal precision.

papers.title: Long-read whole-genome sequencing dataset of microbial communities from industrially and municipally impacted freshwater wetlands in South Africa.
papers.abstract: This article describes a long-read whole-genome shotgun sequencing dataset generated from microbial communities inhabiting industrially and municipally impacted freshwater wetlands in South Africa. Surface water samples were collected from five strategically selected sites exposed to distinct anthropogenic pressures, including industrial effluent discharge, sewage overflow, greywater inputs, informal settlement runoff, and landfill leachate to generate a unique microbial genomic data. Environmental DNA was extracted and sequenced using the PacBio Sequel IIe platform, producing high-fidelity lo
papers.summary: This paper presents a long-read whole-genome sequencing dataset of microbial communities from South African freshwater wetlands impacted by industrial and municipal pollution. The data, generated using PacBio sequencing, provides a genomic resource for studying microbial responses to different anthropogenic stressors.

papers.title: Convergent GenX biodegradation by genomically designed and functionally screened synthetic bacterial consortia.
papers.abstract: GenX (hexafluoropropylene oxide dimer acid) has been introduced as a replacement for legacy polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFASs) compounds; however, accumulating evidence indicates this C6 ether-PFAS may exhibit comparable toxicity to PFOA while persisting in contaminated water supplies. Biological remediation offers genuine mineralization potential but has been constrained by the exceptional stability of carbon-fluorine bonds. Here, we demonstrate that synthetic bacterial consortia assembled through rational genomic design (GENIA) or microfluidic selection (Community G) achieve rapid GenX biode
papers.summary: Researchers developed synthetic bacterial consortia, either through rational genomic design or microfluidic selection, that can rapidly biodegrade GenX, a replacement PFAS compound with potential toxicity similar to PFOA. This work overcomes the challenge of carbon-fluorine bond stability, offering a promising biological remediation approach for contaminated water supplies.

papers.title: From genomes to interventions: computational strategies transforming parasitology.
papers.abstract: In silico techniques have become essential in contemporary parasitology, offering swift, cost-effective, and scalable methods for exploring parasite biology, host-parasite interactions, drug resistance, the discovery of diagnostic markers, vaccine development, and the prioritization of therapeutic targets. Computational frameworks that encompass genomics, pangenomics, phylogenetics, transcriptomics, proteomics, structural biology, molecular docking, molecular dynamics simulations, artificial intelligence (AI), and immunoinformatics have collectively revolutionized parasite research. They enabl
papers.summary: This review highlights how computational methods—including genomics, AI, and structural biology—are transforming parasitology by enabling faster, cheaper discovery of drug targets, vaccines, and diagnostic markers. These in silico approaches provide scalable tools to study parasite biology and host-parasite interactions, accelerating research that was previously limited by experimental costs and complexity.

papers.title: Comparative analysis of morphology and chloroplast genomes in endangered plant Madhuca pasquieri and two congeneric plants: revealing phylogenetic relationships.
papers.abstract: This study addresses the taxonomic ambiguity impeding the conservation of Madhuca pasquieri - an endangered tree species classified as vulnerable on the International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN) Red List and listed as a national second-class protected plant in China - by integrating morphological comparison with complete chloroplast genome analysis of three Madhuca species.
papers.summary: This study clarifies the taxonomic classification of the endangered tree Madhuca pasquieri by comparing its physical traits and complete chloroplast genome with two related species. The findings resolve phylogenetic relationships, which is crucial for guiding conservation efforts for this vulnerable species.

papers.title: Functional characterization and safety evaluation of an airway commensal Staphylococcus epidermidis HK 95.
papers.abstract: The airway epithelium plays a central role in maintaining respiratory homeostasis, and increasing evidence suggests that resident commensal bacteria contribute to airway protection through host-microbe interactions. However, the functional roles of individual airway commensals remain poorly defined. Here, we aimed to isolate and functionally characterize an airway commensal Staphylococcus epidermidis strain and to determine its influence on airway function as well as its safety profile.
papers.summary: This study isolated and characterized a specific strain of Staphylococcus epidermidis from the airway, finding it can interact with host cells without causing harm. The research provides initial evidence that this commensal bacterium may play a protective role in respiratory health, though its functional significance remains to be fully defined.

papers.title: Large-scale comparative genomics and structure-function analysis enables characterization of known and novel genetic determinants of antimicrobial resistance in bacterial pathogens.
papers.abstract: Antibiotics are crucial for preventing infection-induced complications, but their widespread overuse has spurred the evolution of antimicrobial resistance (AMR) mechanisms in pathogens. Data-driven biosurveillance approaches utilizing whole genome sequencing data and computational approaches have the potential to improve the detection and characterization of known and emerging AMR profiles, especially in high-priority ESKAPE, enteric, and sexually-transmitted pathogens.
papers.summary: This study uses large-scale comparative genomics and structure-function analysis to identify both known and novel genetic determinants of antimicrobial resistance (AMR) in bacterial pathogens. By analyzing whole genome sequencing data from high-priority pathogens, the authors demonstrate the potential of data-driven biosurveillance to improve detection and characterization of AMR profiles. The findings highlight the utility of computational approaches in tracking emerging resistance mechanisms.

papers.title: GWAS analysis of a depression cohort defined by an EHR-phenotyping algorithm reveals the role of immune regulations in depression risk.
papers.abstract: Depression is a common psychiatric disorder and a leading cause of disability. Large-scale genomic studies have identified common variants associated with depression. However, researchers often rely on self-reported phenotypes, domain expertise-defined rules, and simple diagnostic codes (ICD9, ICD10) to identify depression participants, which suffers from inconsistent cohort definition and limited sample sizes. Thus, there is a lack of validated, efficient EHR phenotyping algorithms that precisely recognize depression cases.
papers.summary: This study developed an EHR-based phenotyping algorithm to more accurately identify depression cases from electronic health records, then performed a GWAS that revealed immune regulation pathways as significant contributors to depression risk. The approach improves upon traditional methods that rely on self-reports or simple diagnostic codes, enabling more consistent and larger-scale genomic analyses.

papers.title: Somatic mutation profiles in aged military nuclear test veterans: A comparative whole-genome sequence study.
papers.abstract: Veterans of the British nuclear testing programme represent an aged group of ex-military personnel who may have been exposed to ionising radiation through their participation at nuclear testing sites. This study aimed to compare the somatic mutational landscape of a cohort of 30 nuclear test veterans with that of an age-matched cohort of 30 control veterans. Variants were identified from publicly available whole-genome sequencing data using a bioinformatics pipeline developed in accordance with the gold standard approaches as defined by the Broad Institute. The resulting set of raw SNV and IND
papers.summary: This study compared whole-genome sequences from 30 British nuclear test veterans and 30 age-matched controls, finding no significant differences in the number or types of somatic mutations between the two groups. The results suggest that participation in nuclear testing did not leave a distinct mutational signature in the blood cells of these veterans.

papers.title: A Region-Aware Structured Framework Improves Prediction of Gene Expression from DNA Methylation.
papers.abstract: DNA methylation is a key epigenetic modification that plays an important role in gene expression regulation and disease development. Inferring gene expression from DNA methylation provides a computational strategy for cross-omics integration and facilitates the exploration of regulatory relationships between epigenetic modifications and transcription. However, the regulatory effects of methylation on gene expression often exhibit complex characteristics, and methylation in different functional regions of a gene may follow distinct regulatory patterns. Existing methods typically lack the capaci
papers.summary: This paper presents a new computational framework that improves the prediction of gene expression from DNA methylation data by accounting for the distinct regulatory effects of methylation in different functional regions of a gene. The method outperforms existing approaches by capturing complex, region-specific patterns in the relationship between epigenetic modifications and transcription.
